question: A Java job worker is opened with `streamEnabled(true)` and `maxJobsActive = 32`. The worker is already handling 32 jobs when a 33rd job becomes activate-able on the broker. What happens?

options.text: The gRPC stream blocks via built-in flow control, signalling the gateway to stop pushing more jobs until one in-flight job completes.
The 33rd job is dropped on the worker and an incident is raised on the process instance immediately.
The worker silently bypasses `maxJobsActive` because streaming is enabled — the cap only applies to polling.
The 33rd job is buffered in worker memory indefinitely until any of the in-flight jobs finishes.

a.text: With streaming, the Java client relies on built-in gRPC flow control. At `maxJobsActive` capacity the gRPC thread blocks, which signals the gateway to stop sending more jobs until in-flight work drops below the cap.

b.text: Backpressure does not drop jobs at the worker. Jobs are only 'dropped' on the broker side when the worker blocks longer than the job's deadline — and even then, the job is later re-pushed after timing out.

c.text: The docs are explicit that backpressure applies to BOTH pushing and polling when streaming is enabled, so `maxJobsActive` still bounds concurrent in-flight work.

d.text: There is no unbounded memory buffer beyond `maxJobsActive`; the gateway is told to pause sending more.

explanation: Enabling `streamEnabled(true)` opens a long-living stream over which jobs are pushed without polling. `maxJobsActive` remains the soft cap on concurrent in-flight jobs (and a soft bound on memory: roughly `maxJobsActive × maxMessageSize`). When that cap is reached, the gRPC thread blocks, the built-in flow control signals the gateway to stop pushing, and the worker resumes receiving jobs once in-flight work drains.
